narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 29, full-time student status, and your low decile rankings in Year 1 and Year 3 school grades—and calculated a 94.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The three most influential factors in pushing your prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your undergraduate GPA. Your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the typical student's average of around 5.5—had the strongest negative impact, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers who passed the bar. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1, also well below the average of about 5.7, reinforced this concern by highlighting early struggles that often predict bar exam challenges. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, while slightly below the average of 3.21, worked somewhat in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, though not enough to offset the other issues. Overall, your academic deciles stood out as much weaker than those of typical admitted students, whose stronger high school-like rankings correlate with bar success. Your LSAT score of 29, notably lower than the average of 36.7, and middle family income also factored in but were less decisive here. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward or above average, while building a stronger academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program that could improve future decile-like metrics. Consider part-time options if full-time study overwhelmed you before, and gather strong letters of recommendation emphasizing your resilience as a white male from a middle-income background navigating these hurdles. With targeted improvements, you can reapply stronger and shift the model's prediction in your favor.
